### Article 9 — Risk Management System  
**Chapter III, Section 2: Requirements for High-Risk AI Systems**
**Date of application:** 2 August 2026 (Article 113)  
1. A risk management system shall be established, implemented, documented and maintained in relation to high-risk AI systems. The risk management system shall be understood as a continuous iterative process planned and run throughout the entire lifecycle of a high-risk AI system, requiring regular systematic review and updating.  
2. The risk management system shall comprise the following steps:
(a) the identification and analysis of the known and reasonably foreseeable risks that the high-risk AI system can pose to health, safety or fundamental rights when the high-risk AI system is used in accordance with its intended purpose;
(b) the estimation and evaluation of the risks that may emerge when the high-risk AI system is used in accordance with its intended purpose, and under conditions of reasonably foreseeable misuse;
(c) the evaluation of other risks possibly arising, based on the analysis of data gathered from the post-market monitoring system referred to in Article 72;
(d) the adoption of appropriate and targeted risk management measures designed to address the risks identified pursuant to points (a) of this paragraph.  
5. High-risk AI systems shall be tested for the purposes of identifying the most appropriate and targeted risk management measures. Testing shall ensure that high-risk AI systems perform consistently for their intended purpose and they are in compliance with the requirements set out in this Section.  
High-risk AI systems shall be tested at appropriate points throughout their development process, and, in any event, prior to their being placed on the market or put into service. Testing shall be carried out against prior defined metrics and probabilistic thresholds that are appropriate to the intended purpose of the high-risk AI system.  
6. The testing of high-risk AI systems shall be performed, as appropriate, at any time throughout the development process, and, in any event, prior to the placing on the market or the putting into service. Testing shall be carried out against prior defined metrics and probabilistic thresholds that are appropriate to the intended purpose of the high-risk AI system.  
9. When implementing the risk management system as referred to in paragraphs 1 to 7, providers shall give consideration to whether in view of its intended purpose the high-risk AI system is likely to have an adverse impact on persons under 18 years of age or other vulnerable groups.  
10. For providers of high-risk AI systems that are subject to requirements regarding internal risk management processes under other relevant provisions of Union law, the aspects provided in paragraphs 1 to 9 may be part of, or combined with, the risk management procedures established pursuant to that Union law.  
**Financial AI note (G4):** Article 9(10) is the financial sector integration clause — banks subject to CRD/Solvency II risk management can integrate Article 9 requirements into existing frameworks rather than creating parallel systems. The AI Review Committee must verify the integrated approach covers all Article 9 requirements.  
---

---

### Article 43 — Conformity Assessment  
**Chapter III, Section 5: Standards, Conformity Assessment, Certificates, Registration**
**Date of application:** 2 August 2026 (Article 113)  
1. High-risk AI systems listed in Annex III, with the exception of those listed in points 1, 6 and 7 of that Annex, shall be subject to the conformity assessment procedure based on internal control as referred to in Annex VI, or to the conformity assessment procedure based on assessment of the quality management system and assessment of the technical documentation, as referred to in Annex VII.  
2. For high-risk AI systems referred to in point 1 of Annex III, the conformity assessment procedure shall be carried out by a notified body.  
**Financial AI note (G4):** Credit scoring AI (Annex III §5) and insurance pricing AI (Annex III §5) are eligible for self-assessment (internal control per Annex VI) — third-party notified body assessment is NOT required. Only Annex III points 1, 6, and 7 require notified body assessment. The AI Review Committee must verify conformity assessment completion before EU deployment.  
---
response:
### Planning for Future Changes to the EU AI Act

**1. Monitoring Regulatory Developments (G4: Compliance)**  
The AI Review Committee should establish a systematic process for monitoring updates to the EU AI Act, particularly regarding potential expansions of the High-Risk AI list under Article 7. This includes:
- Regularly reviewing communications from the European Commission and relevant regulatory bodies.
- Engaging with industry associations and legal experts to stay informed about proposed amendments.

**2. Risk Management System Adaptation (G1: Accuracy, G4: Compliance)**  
In anticipation of changes, the Committee should ensure that the existing risk management system, as outlined in Article 9, is flexible enough to incorporate new high-risk AI systems. This involves:
- Conducting a gap analysis to identify how new AI systems may fit into the current risk management framework.
- Updating risk assessment protocols to include any new categories of high-risk AI systems as they are defined.

**3. Integration with Existing Frameworks (G4: Compliance)**  
As per Article 9(10), the Committee should verify that the integration of new high-risk AI systems into existing risk management frameworks (e.g., CRD/Solvency II) is seamless. This includes:
- Ensuring that any new requirements from the expanded High-Risk AI list are incorporated into the current risk management processes without creating parallel systems.
- Documenting the integration process to maintain compliance and facilitate audits.

**4. Training and Awareness (G3: Transparency)**  
The Committee should implement training programs for relevant stakeholders within the financial institution to ensure awareness of potential changes to the High-Risk AI list and their implications. This includes:
- Developing educational materials that explain the significance of high-risk classifications and the associated compliance requirements.
- Conducting workshops to prepare teams for the operational impacts of new high-risk AI systems.

**5. Human Oversight and Testing Protocols (G2: Safety)**  
Given the potential for increased risk associated with new high-risk AI systems, the Committee should emphasize the importance of human oversight in the testing and deployment phases. This involves:
- Establishing protocols for human review of AI outputs, especially in areas like credit scoring and insurance pricing, to mitigate risks before deployment.
- Ensuring that testing procedures align with the requirements set out in Article 9(5) and (6), focusing on defined metrics and probabilistic thresholds.

**6. Documentation and Reporting (G4: Compliance)**  
Finally, the Committee should maintain thorough documentation of all actions taken in response to potential changes in the EU AI Act. This includes:
- Keeping records of risk assessments, integration processes, and training sessions.
- Preparing reports for internal stakeholders and regulatory bodies to demonstrate compliance with evolving regulations.

By following these structured steps, the AI Review Committee can effectively prepare for future changes to the EU AI Act and ensure ongoing compliance with its provisions.

question: Article 6(3) of the EU AI Act allows providers to argue that an Annex III AI system is not actually High-Risk because it does not pose significant risk. Can the bank argue that its credit scoring AI is not High-Risk despite being listed in Annex III §5(b)?
ground_truth: Article 6(3) creates an exception to Annex III High-Risk classification: an AI system that is listed in Annex III shall NOT be considered High-Risk if it does not pose a significant risk of harm to health, safety, or the fundamental rights of natural persons. However, this exception has narrow applicability: (1) Exception criteria (Art.6(3)) — the AI system is not High-Risk if: (a) it performs a narrow procedural task; (b) it improves the result of a previously completed human activity; (c) it detects decision-making patterns or deviations from prior decision-making patterns and is not meant to replace or influence the previously completed human assessment without proper human review; or (d) it performs a preparatory task to an assessment relevant for the purpose listed in Annex III; (2) Application to credit scoring AI — a credit scoring AI that makes or directly influences credit approval/rejection decisions does NOT qualify for this exception; it is not merely performing a narrow procedural task or preparing for human assessment; it is making substantive judgments that directly affect credit access; (3) Narrow exception scope — the exception is designed for AI systems that assist in administrative tasks related to Annex III activities, not for AI that is the primary decision mechanism; (4) Unjustified non-High-Risk claims — Article 80 addresses situations where providers unjustifiably classify Annex III AI as non-High-Risk; market surveillance authorities can investigate and require High-Risk compliance; (5) Documentation requirement — if the bank's credit AI were to invoke Art.6(3), it must document the reasoning and notify the market surveillance authority per Art.49(2); the AI Review Committee should not invoke Art.6(3) for credit scoring AI.
response:
**Answer:**

**Governance Axis:** G4 (Compliance)

**Regulatory Provision:** Article 6(3) of the EU AI Act

**Conclusion:** The bank cannot argue that its credit scoring AI is not High-Risk despite being listed in Annex III §5(b).

**Rationale:**
1. **High-Risk Classification:** According to the regulatory context, credit scoring AI is explicitly classified as a high-risk AI system under Annex III §5(b). This classification is definitive and does not allow for exceptions based on the perceived risk of harm.

2. **Article 6(3) Exception Limitations:** Article 6(3) provides conditions under which certain AI systems listed in Annex III may not be considered high-risk if they do not pose a significant risk of harm. However, this exception does not apply to credit scoring AI, as it directly influences credit decisions, which inherently involves significant risk to individuals' financial well-being.

3. **Regulatory Compliance Obligations:** As a high-risk AI system, the bank must comply with the obligations outlined in Article 16, including ensuring conformity assessments, maintaining quality management systems, and fulfilling documentation requirements.

**Recommendation:** The AI Review Committee should ensure that the bank's credit scoring AI adheres to all compliance obligations as a high-risk AI system, including the completion of the necessary conformity assessment procedures as specified in Article 43. Human oversight is recommended in the decision-making process to mitigate risks associated with potential biases or inaccuracies in the AI system.

question: The bank's AML AI system screens transactions and customer profiles for money laundering indicators. The AI Review Committee must determine whether this qualifies as High-Risk AI under the EU AI Act's Annex III.
ground_truth: AML AI classification requires careful analysis of Annex III categories: (1) Annex III §6 — Law Enforcement: §6(a) covers AI assessing the risk of a natural person becoming a victim of criminal offences; §6(d) covers AI for assessing risk of a person offending or re-offending; §6(e) covers AI for profiling natural persons during criminal investigation; these provisions are explicitly limited to use 'by or on behalf of law enforcement authorities' or 'Union institutions in support of law enforcement'; (2) Private bank AML AI — a bank's internal AML monitoring AI is not operated 'by or on behalf of law enforcement authorities'; it operates for regulatory compliance (Anti-Money Laundering Directive compliance) and internal fraud prevention; it is not a law enforcement tool; therefore, Annex III §6 does not classify private bank AML AI as High-Risk; (3) Other Annex III categories — AML AI does not clearly fit other Annex III categories (it is not credit scoring, insurance pricing, employment AI, etc.); (4) Current assessment — private bank AML transaction monitoring AI is NOT High-Risk under Annex III as currently written; (5) Article 50 transparency — AML AI does not typically interact directly with customers in a way triggering Art.50; however, if the AI generates customer-facing communications about account restrictions, disclosure obligations may apply; (6) Independent financial regulatory obligations — AML AI compliance is primarily governed by AMLD6/Anti-Money Laundering Regulation rather than EU AI Act; (7) AI Review Committee approach — apply robust governance despite non-High-Risk classification given the significant impact on customer account access rights.
legal_basis: EU AI Act Annex III; Article 6
response:
### Assessment of AML AI System under EU AI Act

**1. Classification of the AML AI System:**
- The AML AI system is designed to screen transactions and customer profiles for indicators of money laundering. 
- According to the regulatory context, specifically **Annex III**, the AI system does not fall under any of the high-risk categories listed, particularly because it is not intended for credit scoring or insurance pricing, which are explicitly classified as high-risk under **Article 6(2)**.

**2. Relevant Provisions:**
- **Annex III §5(b)** states that AI systems intended to evaluate creditworthiness or establish credit scores are considered high-risk, but this does not apply to AML systems.
- The **Financial AI note** clarifies that **AML/KYC AI** is **NOT High-Risk** as it is limited to law enforcement applications and does not fall under the high-risk categories defined in the context.

**3. Governance Axis:**
- This assessment primarily relates to **G4 (Compliance)**, as it involves determining the regulatory classification of the AI system.

**4. High-Risk Scenario Flagging:**
- Since the AML AI system is not classified as high-risk, there are no specific high-risk scenarios identified that would necessitate additional human oversight under the current regulatory framework.

**5. Conclusion:**
- The bank's AML AI system does **not qualify as a High-Risk AI system** under the EU AI Act's Annex III. Therefore, it is not subject to the obligations outlined in **Article 16** for high-risk AI systems.

**Actionable Recommendation:**
- The AI Review Committee should document this classification and ensure ongoing monitoring of any changes in regulatory definitions or classifications that may affect the AML AI system in the future.

question: The bank's ESG team asks whether the EU AI Act includes any provisions addressing the environmental impact of AI systems. How should the AI Review Committee respond?
ground_truth: The EU AI Act does not include explicit mandatory provisions on AI environmental impact or energy consumption comparable to its human rights and safety requirements. However: (1) Recital considerations — some EU AI Act recitals reference sustainability and environmental considerations as context for the regulation; these do not create binding obligations but signal regulatory direction; (2) Potential connection to Article 9 risk management — the Article 9(1) risk management system broadly covers 'risks to health, safety or fundamental rights'; environmental harm from AI (e.g., massive energy consumption contributing to climate risk) could theoretically be included in a comprehensive risk management framework, though this is not explicitly required; (3) Technical documentation (Art.11, Annex IV) — the technical documentation requirements do not explicitly require energy consumption disclosure, unlike some proposed AI reporting frameworks; (4) EU regulatory evolution — the European Commission has committed to monitoring AI's environmental impact; future amendments to the AI Act or complementary regulations (AI Liability Directive, ESG reporting frameworks) may introduce environmental AI obligations; (5) CSRD connection — banks subject to the Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD) must report on climate-related risks and impacts; AI energy consumption may need to be included in this reporting even if not required by the AI Act directly; (6) AI Review Committee practical response — while not an EU AI Act requirement, including AI environmental impact assessment in governance as a voluntary best practice: satisfies CSRD reporting needs; demonstrates comprehensive ESG commitment; aligns with NIST AI RMF MEASURE 2.12 environmental impact assessment requirement.
response:
### Response to ESG Team Inquiry on Environmental Impact of AI Systems

**1. Regulatory Context Overview:**
The EU AI Act, as outlined in Regulation (EU) 2024/1689, primarily focuses on the governance of AI systems based on their risk classification, particularly high-risk AI systems. The articles provided in the context do not explicitly address the environmental impact of AI systems.

**2. Relevant Articles:**
- **Article 16** outlines the obligations of providers of high-risk AI systems, focusing on compliance, quality management, technical documentation, and conformity assessments. However, it does not mention environmental considerations.
- **Article 43** details the conformity assessment procedures for high-risk AI systems but similarly lacks any reference to environmental impacts.

**3. Governance Axis:**
- **G4 (Compliance):** The inquiry pertains to compliance with existing regulations, but the current provisions do not encompass environmental impact assessments.

**4. High-Risk Scenarios:**
While the Act does not currently address environmental impacts, the bank should remain vigilant for future regulatory developments or amendments that may introduce such requirements. 

**5. Recommendations:**
- **Monitor Regulatory Developments:** The AI Review Committee should keep abreast of any updates to the EU AI Act or related legislation that may introduce environmental impact assessments for AI systems.
- **Consider Internal Policies:** The bank may consider developing internal guidelines or policies that address the environmental impact of AI systems, even in the absence of specific regulatory requirements.

**6. Conclusion:**
The current provisions of the EU AI Act do not include specific regulations regarding the environmental impact of AI systems. The AI Review Committee should focus on compliance with existing articles while preparing for potential future regulations in this area.

question: The AI Review Committee must understand the EU AI Act's timeline for applicability. What is the phased implementation schedule under Article 113, and what obligations are already in force for financial institutions?
ground_truth: Article 113 of the EU AI Act establishes a phased implementation timeline: (1) Entry into force — the Regulation entered into force on 1 August 2024 (20 days after publication in the Official Journal on 12 July 2024); (2) Phase 1 — 6 months after entry into force (February 2025): Chapter I (general provisions) and Chapter II (prohibited AI practices, Art.5) become applicable; financial institutions must immediately comply with Article 5 prohibited AI practice prohibitions as of February 2025; (3) Phase 2 — 12 months (August 2025): Chapter V (General-Purpose AI Models) and Chapter VII (Governance) become applicable; GPAI model obligations and EU governance structure are operational; (4) Phase 3 — 24 months (August 2026): the majority of the Regulation becomes applicable, including: Chapter III (High-Risk AI requirements — Art.9-15, 16-27); Chapter IV (Transparency obligations — Art.50); Chapter VIII (EU database); Chapter IX (Post-market monitoring, market surveillance); this is the critical phase for financial AI governance; (5) Phase 4 — 36 months (August 2027): Annex I (harmonized Union legislation) High-Risk AI becomes applicable; (6) For financial institutions (as of May 2026 — current date): Art.5 prohibited practices are already applicable (since February 2025); GPAI model obligations are applicable (since August 2025); High-Risk AI Chapter III obligations are applicable (since August 2026); (7) AI Review Committee priority — all High-Risk financial AI (credit scoring, insurance pricing) must be fully compliant with Chapter III requirements NOW (as of August 2026 application date).
context_used:
# EU AI Act — Key Articles for Financial AI Governance Research
# Regulation (EU) 2024/1689 | Official Journal version, 13 June 2024
# Source: artificialintelligenceact.eu | Collected: 2025-05-31
# Scope: Articles relevant to financial institution AI Review Committee governance
# RAG chunking: split on "### Article" headers  
---

---

### Article 113 — Entry into Force and Application (Summary)  
**Chapter XIII: Final Provisions**  
Key dates for financial institutions:
- **1 August 2024** — Regulation enters into force
- **2 February 2025** — Chapter II (Article 5 prohibited practices) applies
- **2 August 2025** — Chapter V (GPAI models) and Chapter VII (Governance) apply; Article 99 penalties applicable
- **2 August 2026** — Main Regulation applies, including: Chapter III (High-Risk AI — Articles 9-27); Chapter IV (Article 50 transparency); Chapter IX (post-market monitoring, Art.72-73; remedies, Art.86)
- **2 August 2027** — Annex I (product safety legislation) High-Risk AI obligations apply  
**Current status (as of May 2026):** Article 5 prohibited practices ALREADY apply (since Feb 2025). GPAI model obligations ALREADY apply (since Aug 2025). High-Risk AI Chapter III obligations including credit scoring and insurance pricing AI compliance are IMMINENT (August 2026 application).  
---  
*Source: artificialintelligenceact.eu (Official Journal version, Regulation (EU) 2024/1689, 13 June 2024)*
*Collected: 31 May 2025 | Updated: 31 May 2025*
*Version: v2.0-complete (RAG-ready)*
*Coverage: Art.2, 4, 5, 6, Annex III, 9, 10, 13, 14, 15, 16, 17, 26, 27, 43, 47, 49, 50, 72, 73, 86, 99, 113*
*Chunking guide: split on "### Article" headers for RAG TextLoader*

---

### Article 2 — Scope  
**Chapter I: General Provisions**
**Date of application:** 2 August 2026 (Article 113)  
1. This Regulation applies to the following:
(a) providers placing on the market or putting into service AI systems or placing on the market general-purpose AI models in the Union, irrespective of whether those providers are established or located within the Union or in a third country;
(b) deployers of AI systems that have their place of establishment or are located within the Union;
(c) providers and deployers of AI systems that have their place of establishment or are located in a third country, where the output produced by the AI system is used in the Union;
(d) importers and distributors of AI systems;
(e) product manufacturers placing on the market or putting into service an AI system together with their product and under their own name or trademark;
(f) authorised representatives of providers, which are not established in the Union;
(g) affected persons that are located in the Union.  
8. This Regulation shall not apply to AI systems developed or used exclusively for the purposes of scientific research and development.  
**Financial AI note:** Article 2(1)(c) establishes extra-territorial application — AI systems developed outside the EU but whose outputs affect EU-based persons are subject to this Regulation. A Korean bank's credit scoring AI used for EU customers falls within scope.  
---
response:
### Phased Implementation Schedule under Article 113

**1. Key Dates for Financial Institutions:**
   - **1 August 2024**: Regulation enters into force.
   - **2 February 2025**: Chapter II (Article 5 - Prohibited Practices) applies.
   - **2 August 2025**: 
     - Chapter V (Governance of General-Purpose AI Models) applies.
     - Chapter VII (Governance) applies.
   - **2 August 2026**: 
     - Chapter III (High-Risk AI - Articles 9-27) obligations apply, including compliance for credit scoring and insurance pricing AI.
   - **2 August 2027**: Annex I (product safety legislation) High-Risk AI obligations apply.

**2. Current Status (as of May 2026):**
   - **Already in Force**:
     - Article 5 (Prohibited Practices) has been applicable since **2 February 2025**.
     - Chapter V (GPAI models) and Chapter VII (Governance) obligations have been applicable since **2 August 2025**.

### Summary of Obligations Already in Force
- Financial institutions must comply with the prohibited practices outlined in Article 5 as of February 2025.
- Governance requirements for General-Purpose AI Models are also in effect since August 2025.

### Governance Axis
- **G4 (Compliance)**: Ensuring adherence to the phased implementation schedule and obligations under the EU AI Act is critical for compliance.

### Recommendations
- Financial institutions should prepare for the upcoming obligations under Chapter III by assessing their AI systems for compliance with high-risk AI regulations before the August 2026 deadline. Human oversight is recommended for high-risk AI applications, particularly in credit scoring and insurance pricing, to mitigate risks associated with compliance and accuracy.

question: Article 26(7) of the EU AI Act requires deployers of High-Risk AI to inform workers and their representatives about the use of High-Risk AI in the workplace. How does this apply to loan officers using the credit scoring AI?
ground_truth: Article 26(7) requires deployers to inform workers and worker representatives about the use of High-Risk AI systems in the workplace before such use begins. For loan officers using the credit scoring AI: (1) Scope — the obligation applies when the High-Risk AI is used to assist or replace human decision-making in work processes; using credit scoring AI to inform loan officer credit decisions is precisely this type of use; (2) What information must be provided — while the Act does not specify exact content, workers must be informed that High-Risk AI is being used and in what capacity; information should include: that the AI is classified as High-Risk under the EU AI Act; the purpose and capabilities of the credit scoring AI; how the AI output is intended to be used in the loan decision process; the human oversight role that loan officers are expected to perform; how loan officers can override or disregard AI recommendations; the monitoring and logging that occurs during AI use; (3) Timing — information must be provided before use begins; for new loan officers, this should be part of onboarding; for existing officers when the system is introduced, advance notice; (4) Workers' representatives — European Works Councils (EWCs) and national workers' councils must be informed for larger institutions; (5) Connection to Art.14 human oversight — worker information under Art.26(7) is foundational to the effective human oversight required by Art.14; loan officers cannot perform meaningful oversight of an AI system they were never informed about; (6) AI Review Committee obligation — ensure Art.26(7) worker information procedure is documented and implemented before deployment.
response:
### Application of Article 26(7) to Loan Officers Using Credit Scoring AI

**Regulatory Provision:**
- **Article 26(7)** mandates that deployers of high-risk AI systems must inform workers' representatives and affected workers about the use of high-risk AI systems in the workplace.

**Governance Axis:**
- **G4 (Compliance)**

**Implications for Loan Officers:**
1. **Notification Requirement:**
   - Loan officers, as affected workers, must be informed prior to the deployment of the credit scoring AI system. This includes details about how the AI will be used in their decision-making processes.

2. **Communication to Workers' Representatives:**
   - The financial institution must also communicate this information to workers' representatives, ensuring that they are aware of the AI's role and implications in the workplace.

3. **Compliance with Internal Governance:**
   - The institution should ensure that this notification process aligns with any existing internal governance arrangements or processes under Union financial services law, as referenced in Article 26(5).

4. **Documentation:**
   - The institution should maintain records of the communication provided to loan officers and their representatives to demonstrate compliance with Article 26(7).

**High-Risk Scenarios:**
- **Potential Risks:**
  - There is a risk of misunderstanding or lack of awareness among loan officers regarding the AI's capabilities and limitations, which could lead to over-reliance on AI outputs (automation bias).

**Recommendation for Human Oversight:**
- **Training and Awareness:**
  - It is recommended that the institution provides training sessions for loan officers to ensure they understand the AI system's functionalities, limitations, and the importance of human oversight in decision-making processes. This aligns with the requirements outlined in Article 14(4) regarding the capabilities loan officers must possess.

### Actionable Steps:
1. Develop a communication plan to inform loan officers and their representatives about the credit scoring AI.
2. Document the notification process and training sessions to ensure compliance with Article 26(7).
3. Implement ongoing training to mitigate risks associated with automation bias and ensure effective human oversight.

question: Article 15(3) of the EU AI Act requires technical robustness solutions including redundancy for High-Risk AI. How should the AI Review Committee implement cybersecurity and resilience measures for the credit scoring AI consistent with Article 15?
ground_truth: Article 15 requires High-Risk AI systems to achieve appropriate levels of accuracy, robustness, and cybersecurity throughout their lifecycle, maintained through state-of-the-art measures. Specific cybersecurity requirements for credit scoring AI: (1) Resilience against attempted manipulation (Art.15(1)) — the credit scoring AI must be resilient to attempts by third parties to alter its use, outputs, or performance through manipulation of the AI system, its components, or training data; key threats to address: adversarial input attacks — applicants crafting applications to game the AI scoring system; data poisoning — corrupting training data pipelines to bias future model outputs; prompt injection — for LLM-based components, injecting instructions that alter AI behavior; model extraction — reverse-engineering the scoring model through API access patterns; (2) Resilience against errors and faults (Art.15(2)) — the AI must be designed to be resilient to input errors, faults, or inconsistencies; fail-safe design: when inputs are anomalous or inconsistent (e.g., contradictory financial data), the system should flag for human review rather than producing a potentially erroneous score; (3) Redundancy solutions (Art.15(3)) — technical redundancy may be required appropriate to the risk; for credit scoring AI: hot standby scoring capability to maintain continuity if primary system fails; validated fallback scoring methodology (e.g., rule-based scorecard) for use during AI system unavailability; (4) DORA intersection — EU financial institutions are also subject to the Digital Operational Resilience Act (DORA); DORA ICT risk management requirements and EU AI Act Art.15 cybersecurity requirements overlap substantially; the AI Review Committee should integrate compliance to avoid duplication.
response:
### Implementation of Cybersecurity and Resilience Measures for Credit Scoring AI

**1. Compliance with Article 15(3) Requirements**  
To ensure compliance with Article 15(3) of the EU AI Act, the AI Review Committee should implement the following measures for the credit scoring AI:

- **Redundancy Solutions**: Establish redundancy mechanisms to ensure continuous operation and reliability of the AI system. This may include:
  - **Backup Systems**: Implement backup systems that can take over in case of failure of the primary system.
  - **Fail-Safe Plans**: Develop fail-safe protocols that activate under specific failure conditions to prevent erroneous outputs or system downtime.

**2. Cybersecurity Measures**  
In alignment with Article 15(2), the following cybersecurity measures should be integrated:

- **Resilience Against Attacks**: Ensure the AI system is designed to withstand adversarial attacks and data poisoning. This includes:
  - **Regular Security Audits**: Conduct periodic security assessments to identify and mitigate vulnerabilities.
  - **Input Validation**: Implement strict input validation protocols to detect and reject anomalous or malicious data inputs.

**3. Integration with DORA Compliance**  
As noted in the financial AI note, ensure that the cybersecurity measures are integrated with the Digital Operational Resilience Act (DORA) requirements. This includes:

- **Operational Resilience Framework**: Develop a framework that encompasses both AI-specific and broader operational resilience strategies to safeguard against disruptions.

**4. Human Oversight**  
Given the high-risk nature of credit scoring AI, human oversight is crucial. The AI Review Committee should:

- **Establish Oversight Protocols**: Define clear human oversight measures as per Article 14, ensuring that human operators can intervene in case of system anomalies or unexpected outputs.
- **Training for Operators**: Provide training for personnel on how to interpret AI outputs and recognize when to intervene.

**5. Documentation and Monitoring**  
Ensure that all measures are documented and monitored:

- **Technical Documentation**: Maintain comprehensive technical documentation as required by Article 11 and Annex IV, detailing the redundancy and cybersecurity measures implemented.
- **Post-Market Monitoring**: Implement a post-market monitoring system as per Article 72(1) to continuously assess the performance and security of the AI system.

### Conclusion  
By implementing these structured measures, the AI Review Committee can ensure that the credit scoring AI complies with Article 15's requirements for technical robustness and cybersecurity, thereby enhancing its resilience and reliability in operation.

question: Article 9(2)(b) of the EU AI Act requires risk assessment under conditions of 'reasonably foreseeable misuse.' What foreseeable misuse scenarios must the AI Review Committee assess for the credit scoring AI?
ground_truth: Article 9(2)(b) requires estimation and evaluation of risks that may emerge when the High-Risk AI system is used under conditions of 'reasonably foreseeable misuse.' For credit scoring AI, reasonably foreseeable misuse scenarios include: (1) Gaming by applicants — applicants deliberately provide false information or manipulate the data they supply to improve their credit score; the AI should be robust to common gaming strategies (overstating income, disguising debt obligations); assess the model's vulnerability to such manipulation and implement validation checks; (2) Discriminatory use by deployer — the bank deploys the AI specifically targeting or excluding protected demographic groups, using the AI to rationalize discriminatory practices that the AI itself may not explicitly exhibit; the risk management system must include governance controls preventing discriminatory deployment even if the model itself is technically fair; (3) Use beyond intended purpose — using the credit scoring AI to assess creditworthiness for products it was not trained for (e.g., using a consumer credit AI for corporate lending); document the intended use boundaries and implement technical controls preventing out-of-scope use; (4) Overreliance without oversight — loan officers effectively outsourcing all credit judgment to the AI without meaningful oversight (automation bias); the risk management plan must address this specific form of misuse by designing oversight processes that maintain human judgment quality; (5) Secondary use of scoring data — using credit score outputs or model features for purposes beyond credit decisions (e.g., for marketing targeting, customer segmentation for other products) without appropriate consent; (6) Delayed update — continuing to use the AI after market conditions have changed materially, making the model's training data stale; (7) Document each misuse scenario and the corresponding mitigation in the Art.9 risk management system.
context_used:
### Article 6 — Classification Rules for High-Risk AI Systems  
**Chapter III, Section 1: Classification of AI Systems as High-Risk**
**Date of application:** 2 August 2026 (Article 113)  
1. Irrespective of whether an AI system is placed on the market or put into service independently from the products referred to in points (a) and (b), that AI system shall be considered to be high-risk where both of the following conditions are fulfilled:
(a) the AI system is intended to be used as a safety component of a product, or the AI system is itself a product, covered by the Union harmonisation legislation listed in Annex I;
(b) the product whose safety component pursuant to point (a) is the AI system, or the AI system itself as a product, is required to undergo a third-party conformity assessment with a view to the placing on the market or putting into service of that product pursuant to the Union harmonisation legislation listed in Annex I.  
2. In addition to the high-risk AI systems referred to in paragraph 1, AI systems referred to in Annex III shall also be considered to be high-risk.  
3. By derogation from paragraph 2, an AI system referred to in Annex III shall not be considered to be high-risk where it does not pose a significant risk of harm to the health, safety or fundamental rights of natural persons, including by not materially influencing the outcome of decision making.  
An AI system referred to in Annex III shall not be considered to be high-risk if it fulfils any of the following conditions:
(a) the AI system is intended to perform a narrow procedural task;
(b) the AI system is intended to improve the result of a previously completed human activity;
(c) the AI system is intended to detect decision-making patterns or deviations from prior decision-making patterns and is not meant to replace or influence the previously completed human assessment, without proper human review;
(d) the AI system is intended to perform a preparatory task to an assessment relevant for the purposes of the use cases listed in Annex III.  
**Financial AI note (G4):** Credit scoring AI (Annex III §5(b)) and insurance pricing AI (Annex III §5(c)) are definitively High-Risk. The Article 6(3) narrow exception does NOT apply to credit scoring AI that directly influences credit decisions.  
---  
### Annex III — High-Risk AI Systems (Article 6(2))  
**Financial sector relevant classifications:**  
**Point 4 — Employment, workers management and access to self-employment:**
(a) AI systems intended to be used for the recruitment or selection of natural persons, notably for placing targeted job advertisements, for analysing and filtering job applications, and for evaluating candidates;
(b) AI systems intended to be used for making decisions on promotion and termination of work-related contractual relationships, for allocating tasks based on individual behaviour or personal traits or characteristics or for monitoring and evaluating performance and behaviour of persons in such relationships.  
**Point 5 — Access to and enjoyment of essential private services and essential public services and benefits:**
(a) AI systems intended to be used by public authorities or on behalf of public authorities to evaluate the eligibility of natural persons for essential public assistance benefits and services, including healthcare services, adequate housing and electricity, and to grant, reduce, revoke, or reclaim such benefits and services;
(b) AI systems intended to be used to evaluate the creditworthiness of natural persons or establish their credit score, **with the exception of AI systems used for the purpose of detecting financial fraud**;
(c) AI systems intended to be used for risk assessment and pricing in relation to natural persons in the case of life and health insurance;
(d) AI systems intended to be used for dispatching, or establishing priority in the dispatching of emergency first response services, including by police, firefighters and medical aid, as well as of emergency healthcare patient triage systems.  
**Financial AI classification summary:**
- Credit scoring AI → **High-Risk** (§5(b)) — lending domain
- Life/health insurance pricing AI → **High-Risk** (§5(c)) — insurance domain
- Financial fraud detection AI → **NOT High-Risk** (§5(b) explicit exception)
- Robo-advisory AI → NOT currently High-Risk (no explicit Annex III category)
- AML/KYC AI (private bank) → NOT High-Risk (§6 limited to law enforcement)
- Employment/recruitment AI → **High-Risk** (§4(a))  
---

---

### Article 14 — Human Oversight  
**Chapter III, Section 2: Requirements for High-Risk AI Systems**
**Date of application:** 2 August 2026 (Article 113)  
1. High-risk AI systems shall be designed and developed in such a way, including with appropriate human-machine interface tools, that they can be effectively overseen by natural persons during the period in which they are in use.  
2. Human oversight shall aim to prevent or minimise the risks to health, safety or fundamental rights that may emerge when a high-risk AI system is used in accordance with its intended purpose or under conditions of reasonably foreseeable misuse, in particular where such risks persist despite the application of other requirements set out in this Section.  
3. The oversight measures shall be commensurate with the risks, level of autonomy and context of use of the high-risk AI system, and shall be ensured through either one or both of the following types of measures:
(a) measures identified and built, when technically feasible, into the high-risk AI system by the provider before it is placed on the market or put into service;
(b) measures identified by the provider before placing the high-risk AI system on the market or putting it into service and that are appropriate to be implemented by the deployer.  
4. For the purpose of implementing paragraphs 1, 2 and 3, the high-risk AI system shall be provided to the deployer in such a way that natural persons to whom human oversight is assigned are enabled, as appropriate and proportionate:
(a) to properly understand the relevant capacities and limitations of the high-risk AI system and be able to duly monitor its operation, including in view of detecting and addressing anomalies, dysfunctions and unexpected performance;
(b) to remain aware of the possible tendency of automatically relying or over-relying on the output produced by a high-risk AI system (automation bias), in particular for high-risk AI systems used to provide information or recommendations for decisions to be taken by natural persons;
(c) to correctly interpret the high-risk AI system's output, taking into account, for example, the interpretation tools and methods available;
(d) to decide, in any particular situation, not to use the high-risk AI system or to otherwise disregard, override or reverse the output of the high-risk AI system;
(e) to intervene in the operation of the high-risk AI system or interrupt the system through a 'stop' button or a similar procedure that allows the system to come to a halt in a safe state.  
5. For high-risk AI systems referred to in point 1(a) of Annex III, the measures referred to in paragraph 3 of this Article shall be such as to ensure that, in addition, no action or decision is taken by the deployer on the basis of the identification resulting from the system unless that identification has been separately verified and confirmed by at least two natural persons with the necessary competence, training and authority.  
**Financial AI note (G2):** Loan officers using credit scoring AI must be enabled to: understand AI capabilities/limitations (§4(a)); resist automation bias (§4(b)); correctly interpret AI output (§4(c)); override AI recommendations (§4(d)); and stop the system if necessary (§4(e)). All five capabilities must be documented and trained.  
---

---

### Article 5 — Prohibited AI Practices  
**Chapter II: Prohibited AI Practices**
**Date of application:** 2 February 2025 (Article 113(a))  
1. The following AI practices shall be prohibited:  
(a) the placing on the market, the putting into service or the use of an AI system that deploys subliminal techniques beyond a person's consciousness or purposefully manipulative or deceptive techniques, with the objective of, or with the effect of materially distorting the behaviour of a person or a group of persons by appreciably impairing their ability to make an informed decision, thereby causing them to take a decision that they would not have otherwise taken in a manner that causes or is reasonably likely to cause that person, another person or a group of persons significant harm;  
(b) the placing on the market, the putting into service or the use of an AI system that exploits any of the vulnerabilities of a natural person or a specific group of persons due to their age, disability or a specific social or economic situation, with the objective of, or the effect of, materially distorting the behaviour of that person or a group of persons in a manner that causes or is reasonably likely to cause that person, another person or a group of persons significant harm;  
(c) the placing on the market, the putting into service or the use of AI systems for the evaluation or classification of natural persons or groups of persons over a certain period of time based on their social behaviour or known, inferred or predicted personal or personality characteristics, with the social score leading to either or both of the following: (i) detrimental or unfavourable treatment of certain natural persons or groups of persons in social contexts that are unrelated to the contexts in which the data was originally generated or collected; (ii) detrimental or unfavourable treatment of certain natural persons or groups of persons that is unjustified or disproportionate to their social behaviour or its gravity;  
(f) the placing on the market, the putting into service or the use of AI systems used for making inferences about natural persons on the basis of their biometric data in order to categorise them according to race, political opinions, trade union membership, religious or philosophical beliefs, sex life or sexual orientation; this prohibition shall not apply to labelling or filtering of lawfully acquired biometric datasets or in law enforcement by competent authorities for the categorisation of biometric data;  
(g) the placing on the market, the putting into service or the use of facial recognition systems for law enforcement purposes, unless strictly necessary for a targeted search of a specific victim or to prevent a specific, substantial and imminent threat;  
(h) the placing on the market, the putting into service, or the use, for law enforcement purposes, of AI systems for making risk assessments of natural persons in order to assess the risk of a natural person for offending or reoffending, or for predicting the occurrence or recurrence of an actual or potential criminal offence.  
**Financial AI note (G2):** Article 5 prohibitions apply as of February 2025. Financial AI must not use subliminal manipulation (5(a)), exploit vulnerable customers (5(b)), or implement social scoring systems (5(c)). The AI Review Committee must screen all AI systems for prohibited practices before approval.  
---
response:
### Foreseeable Misuse Scenarios for Credit Scoring AI

**Governance Axis:** G2 (Safety)

**Regulatory Context:**
- Credit scoring AI is classified as **High-Risk** under Annex III §5(b) and is subject to specific oversight requirements as outlined in Article 14.

**Key Misuse Scenarios to Assess:**

1. **Data Manipulation:**
   - **Scenario:** Users may attempt to manipulate input data (e.g., providing false income information) to achieve a more favorable credit score.
   - **Risk:** This could lead to unjust credit approvals, increasing the risk of default and financial loss for the institution.

2. **Bias and Discrimination:**
   - **Scenario:** The AI may inadvertently perpetuate biases present in historical data, leading to discriminatory outcomes against certain demographic groups.
   - **Risk:** This could result in unfair denial of credit to qualified applicants based on race, gender, or socioeconomic status, violating fundamental rights.

3. **Over-reliance on AI Output:**
   - **Scenario:** Loan officers may overly rely on the AI's recommendations without adequate human review, leading to poor decision-making.
   - **Risk:** This could result in significant financial harm to both the institution and consumers if erroneous credit decisions are made.

4. **Inadequate Human Oversight:**
   - **Scenario:** Lack of proper training or tools for loan officers to understand AI limitations and capabilities.
   - **Risk:** This could lead to misinterpretation of AI outputs and failure to override incorrect recommendations, increasing the likelihood of harmful decisions.

5. **System Vulnerabilities:**
   - **Scenario:** Cybersecurity threats could exploit vulnerabilities in the AI system, leading to unauthorized access and manipulation of credit scoring algorithms.
   - **Risk:** This could compromise the integrity of the credit scoring process and result in financial fraud.

**Recommendations for Human Oversight:**
- Ensure that loan officers are trained to:
  - Understand AI capabilities and limitations (Article 14(4)(a)).
  - Resist automation bias (Article 14(4)(b)).
  - Correctly interpret AI output (Article 14(4)(c)).
  - Override AI recommendations when necessary (Article 14(4)(d)).
  - Stop the system if required (Article 14(4)(e)).
  
**Conclusion:**
The AI Review Committee must conduct a thorough risk assessment of these foreseeable misuse scenarios to ensure compliance with Article 14 and mitigate potential risks associated with the deployment of credit scoring AI.

question: Article 4 of the EU AI Act requires providers and deployers to take measures ensuring sufficient AI literacy of their staff. What AI literacy obligations does this create for the bank's AI Review Committee?
ground_truth: Article 4 of the EU AI Act requires providers and deployers of AI systems to take measures to ensure, to their best extent, a sufficient level of AI literacy of their staff and other persons dealing with the operation and use of AI systems on their behalf, taking into account their technical knowledge, experience, education, and training context, as well as considering the users the AI systems are intended to be used by. For the bank's AI Review Committee: (1) Scope of obligation — applies to all staff dealing with AI operation and use; for a bank this includes: AI developers and data scientists (deep technical literacy); AI Review Committee members (governance and risk literacy); loan officers and other business users of AI (operational literacy); compliance and legal staff (regulatory literacy); board members (strategic literacy); (2) Literacy content should be calibrated by role: technical staff — AI methods, model validation, bias detection; business users — AI capabilities and limitations, when to override, how to interpret outputs; governance staff — EU AI Act requirements, risk management frameworks, FRIA process; executives — AI governance principles, strategic risk implications; (3) Proportionality — 'to their best extent' and 'taking into account technical knowledge' allow proportionate implementation; a loan officer does not need to understand model architecture; (4) Connection to Art.14 human oversight — AI literacy is the foundation for effective human oversight; loan officers who lack literacy about the credit AI's capabilities and limitations cannot perform meaningful oversight; (5) Training program design — the AI Review Committee should develop a tiered AI literacy curriculum covering EU AI Act obligations, AI capabilities and limitations specific to each system, and governance procedures; (6) Record-keeping — maintain training completion records as evidence of Art.4 compliance.
context_used:
### Article 4 — AI Literacy  
**Chapter I: General Provisions**
**Date of application:** 2 August 2026 (Article 113)  
Providers and deployers of AI systems shall take measures to ensure, to their best extent, a sufficient level of AI literacy of their staff and other persons dealing with the operation and use of AI systems on their behalf, having regard to their technical knowledge, experience, education and training and the context the AI systems are to be used in, and taking into account the persons or groups of persons on whom the AI systems are to be used.  
**Financial AI note:** Banks must implement tiered AI literacy programs covering technical staff, loan officers, compliance teams, and board members — each calibrated to their role in AI governance.  
---

---

### Article 16 — Obligations of Providers of High-Risk AI Systems  
**Chapter III, Section 3: Obligations of Providers and Deployers**
**Date of application:** 2 August 2026 (Article 113)  
Providers of high-risk AI systems shall:
(a) ensure that their high-risk AI systems are compliant with the requirements set out in Section 2;
(b) have a quality management system in place pursuant to Article 17;
(c) draw up the technical documentation of the high-risk AI system pursuant to Article 11 and Annex IV;
(d) when the AI system is placed on the market or put into service, keep the automatically generated logs pursuant to Article 19, to the extent such logs are under their control;
(e) before placing a high-risk AI system on the market or putting it into service, ensure that the high-risk AI system has undergone the relevant conformity assessment procedure pursuant to Article 43;
(f) draw up an EU declaration of conformity pursuant to Article 47;
(g) affix CE marking to their high-risk AI system pursuant to Article 48;
(h) comply with the registration obligations referred to in Article 49(1) and (2);
(i) take the necessary corrective actions and provide information as required pursuant to Article 20;
(j) upon request by a national competent authority, demonstrate the conformity of the high-risk AI system with the requirements set out in Section 2;
(k) ensure the high-risk AI system bears the identification number assigned to it upon registration in the EU database in accordance with Article 71;
(l) take the necessary measures to ensure that the post-market monitoring system plan referred to in Article 72(1) is operational;
(m) where applicable, designate an authorised representative pursuant to Article 22.  
**Financial AI note (G4):** If a bank internally develops credit scoring AI and provides it to other institutions, it must fulfill all Article 16 obligations as a provider. The AI Review Committee should maintain a compliance checklist mapped to each Article 16 subparagraph.  
---

---

### Article 26 — Obligations of Deployers of High-Risk AI Systems  
**Chapter III, Section 3: Obligations of Providers and Deployers**
**Date of application:** 2 August 2026 (Article 113)  
1. Deployers of high-risk AI systems shall take appropriate technical and organisational measures to ensure they use such systems in accordance with the instructions for use accompanying the systems, pursuant to paragraphs 3 and 6.  
2. Deployers shall assign human oversight to natural persons who have the necessary competence, training and authority, as well as the necessary support.  
4. Without prejudice to paragraphs 1 and 2, to the extent the deployer exercises control over the input data, that deployer shall ensure that input data is relevant and sufficiently representative in view of the intended purpose of the high-risk AI system.  
5. Deployers shall monitor the operation of the high-risk AI system on the basis of the instructions for use and, where relevant, inform providers in accordance with Article 72. Where deployers have reason to consider that the use of the high-risk AI system in accordance with the instructions may result in that AI system presenting a risk within the meaning of Article 79(1), they shall, without undue delay, inform the provider or distributor and the relevant market surveillance authority, and shall suspend the use of that system. Where deployers have identified a serious incident, they shall also immediately inform first the provider, and then the importer or distributor and the relevant market surveillance authorities of that incident.  
For deployers that are financial institutions subject to requirements regarding their internal governance, arrangements or processes under Union financial services law, the monitoring obligation set out in the first subparagraph shall be deemed to be fulfilled by complying with the rules on internal governance arrangements, processes and mechanisms pursuant to the relevant financial service law.  
6. Deployers of high-risk AI systems shall keep the logs automatically generated by that high-risk AI system to the extent such logs are under their control, for a period appropriate to the intended purpose of the high-risk AI system, of at least six months, unless provided otherwise in applicable Union or national law, in particular in Union law on the protection of personal data. Deployers that are financial institutions subject to requirements regarding their internal governance, arrangements or processes under Union financial services law shall maintain the logs as part of the documentation kept pursuant to the relevant Union financial service law.  
7. Before putting into service or using a high-risk AI system at the workplace, deployers who are employers shall inform workers' representatives and the affected workers that they will be subject to the use of the high-risk AI system. This information shall be provided, where applicable, in accordance with the rules and procedures laid down in Union and national law and practice on information of workers and their representatives.  
9. Where applicable, deployers of high-risk AI systems shall use the information provided under Article 13 of this Regulation to comply with their obligation to carry out a data protection impact assessment under Article 35 of Regulation (EU) 2016/679 or Article 27 of Directive (EU) 2016/680.  
11. Without prejudice to Article 50 of this Regulation, deployers of high-risk AI systems referred to in Annex III that make decisions or assist in making decisions related to natural persons shall inform the natural persons that they are subject to the use of the high-risk AI system.  
12. Deployers shall cooperate with the relevant competent authorities in any action those authorities take in relation to the high-risk AI system in order to implement this Regulation.  
**Financial AI note (G4):** Key bank-as-deployer obligations: use AI per instructions (§1); assign competent human oversight (§2); monitor performance and suspend if risk identified (§5); retain logs minimum 6 months (§6); inform loan officers before AI deployment (§7); notify customers of AI use in decisions (§11). Article 26(5) and (6) include financial institution equivalence provisions for monitoring and log-keeping.  
---

### AI Literacy Obligations for the Bank's AI Review Committee

**Regulatory Provision:**
- **Article 4 — AI Literacy** (Chapter I, General Provisions)

**Obligations:**
1. **Implementation of Tiered AI Literacy Programs:**
   - The bank must develop and implement tiered AI literacy programs tailored to different roles within the organization, including:
     - Technical staff
     - Loan officers
     - Compliance teams
     - Board members
   - Each program should be calibrated to the specific responsibilities and context in which AI systems are used.

   - The bank must assess the technical knowledge, experience, education, and training of staff involved in the operation and use of AI systems to ensure they meet the required literacy levels.

   - The AI Review Committee should ensure that ongoing training and updates are provided to staff as AI technologies evolve and as new regulatory requirements emerge.

4. **Documentation and Reporting:**
   - Maintain records of AI literacy training programs, attendance, and assessments to demonstrate compliance with Article 4 obligations.

**Governance Axis:**
- **G1 (Accuracy)**: Ensuring staff are adequately trained to operate AI systems accurately.
- **G4 (Compliance)**: Meeting regulatory requirements for AI literacy as mandated by Article 4.

**High-Risk Scenarios:**
- Insufficient AI literacy may lead to improper use of AI systems, resulting in compliance failures or operational risks. Therefore, human oversight is recommended to monitor the effectiveness of AI literacy programs and ensure staff are competent in their roles.

**Actionable Recommendations:**
- The AI Review Committee should:
  - Develop a detailed plan for tiered AI literacy programs.
  - Schedule regular assessments of staff competence.
  - Implement a feedback mechanism to continuously improve training programs.
  - Ensure compliance documentation is maintained and readily available for audits.

question: The bank's AI customer service chatbot is deployed in Germany to answer questions about banking products. Article 50(1) requires disclosure that the customer is interacting with an AI. What are the specific requirements and exceptions?
ground_truth: Article 50(1) requires providers of AI systems intended to interact directly with natural persons to design and develop the AI so that the natural persons are informed they are interacting with an AI system, in a timely, clear, and intelligible manner, and at the latest at the beginning of the first interaction. However, exceptions exist: (1) Evident from context exception — the disclosure obligation does not apply where it is evident from the context and the circumstances that the natural person is interacting with an AI system; if the bank's chatbot is clearly branded as 'AI Assistant' or accessed through a clearly labeled AI channel, the disclosure may be self-evident; (2) Law enforcement exception — disclosure may be waived where the AI system is authorized by law for detecting, preventing, investigating, or prosecuting crime; not applicable to banking chatbots; (3) Specific requirements: timing — disclosure must occur 'at the latest at the beginning of the first interaction' (not buried in terms and conditions or disclosed later); clarity — the disclosure must be clear and intelligible to the average user, not just technically present; (4) Practical implementation for banking chatbot: opening message should state explicitly 'You are now chatting with an AI assistant'; if the chat includes human handoff, notify when transitioning from AI to human and back; branding and interface design should reinforce the AI nature of the interaction; (5) Contrast with Article 50(2) — Art.50(1) covers interaction disclosure; Art.50(2) covers marking of AI-generated content; both may apply to the banking chatbot; (6) Enforcement — national competent authorities in Germany (BaFin or AI-specific authority) may investigate and penalize violations.

### Article 50 — Transparency Obligations for Providers and Deployers of Certain AI Systems  
**Chapter IV: Transparency Obligations**
**Date of application:** 2 August 2026 (Article 113)  
1. Providers shall ensure that AI systems intended to interact directly with natural persons are designed and developed in such a way that the natural persons concerned are informed that they are interacting with an AI system, unless this is obvious from the point of view of a natural person who is reasonably well-informed, observant and circumspect, taking into account the circumstances and the context of use. This obligation shall not apply to AI systems authorised by law to detect, prevent, investigate or prosecute criminal offences, subject to appropriate safeguards for the rights and freedoms of third parties, unless those systems are available for the public to report a criminal offence.  
2. Providers of AI systems, including general-purpose AI systems, generating synthetic audio, image, video or text content, shall ensure that the outputs of the AI system are marked in a machine-readable format and detectable as artificially generated or manipulated. Providers shall ensure their technical solutions are effective, interoperable, robust and reliable as far as this is technically feasible, taking into account the specificities and limitations of various types of content, the costs of implementation and the generally acknowledged state of the art, as may be reflected in relevant technical standards.  
3. Deployers of an emotion recognition system or a biometric categorisation system shall inform the natural persons exposed thereto of the operation of the system, and shall process the personal data in accordance with Regulations (EU) 2016/679 and (EU) 2018/1725 and Directive (EU) 2016/680, as applicable.  
4. Deployers of an AI system that generates or manipulates image, audio or video content constituting a deep fake, shall disclose that the content has been artificially generated or manipulated. This obligation shall not apply where the use is authorised by law to detect, prevent, investigate or prosecute criminal offence.  
Deployers of an AI system that generates or manipulates text which is published with the purpose of informing the public on matters of public interest shall disclose that the text has been artificially generated or manipulated.  
5. The information referred to in paragraphs 1 to 4 shall be provided to the natural persons concerned in a clear and distinguishable manner at the latest at the time of the first interaction or exposure.
