# EU AI Act — Key Articles for Financial AI Governance Research
# Regulation (EU) 2024/1689 | Official Journal version, 13 June 2024
# Source: artificialintelligenceact.eu | Collected: 2025-05-31
# Scope: Articles relevant to financial institution AI Review Committee governance
# RAG chunking: split on "### Article" headers  
---

---

### Article 26 — Obligations of Deployers of High-Risk AI Systems  
**Chapter III, Section 3: Obligations of Providers and Deployers**
**Date of application:** 2 August 2026 (Article 113)  
1. Deployers of high-risk AI systems shall take appropriate technical and organisational measures to ensure they use such systems in accordance with the instructions for use accompanying the systems, pursuant to paragraphs 3 and 6.  
2. Deployers shall assign human oversight to natural persons who have the necessary competence, training and authority, as well as the necessary support.  
4. Without prejudice to paragraphs 1 and 2, to the extent the deployer exercises control over the input data, that deployer shall ensure that input data is relevant and sufficiently representative in view of the intended purpose of the high-risk AI system.  
5. Deployers shall monitor the operation of the high-risk AI system on the basis of the instructions for use and, where relevant, inform providers in accordance with Article 72. Where deployers have reason to consider that the use of the high-risk AI system in accordance with the instructions may result in that AI system presenting a risk within the meaning of Article 79(1), they shall, without undue delay, inform the provider or distributor and the relevant market surveillance authority, and shall suspend the use of that system. Where deployers have identified a serious incident, they shall also immediately inform first the provider, and then the importer or distributor and the relevant market surveillance authorities of that incident.  
For deployers that are financial institutions subject to requirements regarding their internal governance, arrangements or processes under Union financial services law, the monitoring obligation set out in the first subparagraph shall be deemed to be fulfilled by complying with the rules on internal governance arrangements, processes and mechanisms pursuant to the relevant financial service law.  
6. Deployers of high-risk AI systems shall keep the logs automatically generated by that high-risk AI system to the extent such logs are under their control, for a period appropriate to the intended purpose of the high-risk AI system, of at least six months, unless provided otherwise in applicable Union or national law, in particular in Union law on the protection of personal data. Deployers that are financial institutions subject to requirements regarding their internal governance, arrangements or processes under Union financial services law shall maintain the logs as part of the documentation kept pursuant to the relevant Union financial service law.  
7. Before putting into service or using a high-risk AI system at the workplace, deployers who are employers shall inform workers' representatives and the affected workers that they will be subject to the use of the high-risk AI system. This information shall be provided, where applicable, in accordance with the rules and procedures laid down in Union and national law and practice on information of workers and their representatives.  
9. Where applicable, deployers of high-risk AI systems shall use the information provided under Article 13 of this Regulation to comply with their obligation to carry out a data protection impact assessment under Article 35 of Regulation (EU) 2016/679 or Article 27 of Directive (EU) 2016/680.  
11. Without prejudice to Article 50 of this Regulation, deployers of high-risk AI systems referred to in Annex III that make decisions or assist in making decisions related to natural persons shall inform the natural persons that they are subject to the use of the high-risk AI system.  
12. Deployers shall cooperate with the relevant competent authorities in any action those authorities take in relation to the high-risk AI system in order to implement this Regulation.  
**Financial AI note (G4):** Key bank-as-deployer obligations: use AI per instructions (§1); assign competent human oversight (§2); monitor performance and suspend if risk identified (§5); retain logs minimum 6 months (§6); inform loan officers before AI deployment (§7); notify customers of AI use in decisions (§11). Article 26(5) and (6) include financial institution equivalence provisions for monitoring and log-keeping.  
---

---

### Article 50 — Transparency Obligations for Providers and Deployers of Certain AI Systems  
**Chapter IV: Transparency Obligations**
**Date of application:** 2 August 2026 (Article 113)  
1. Providers shall ensure that AI systems intended to interact directly with natural persons are designed and developed in such a way that the natural persons concerned are informed that they are interacting with an AI system, unless this is obvious from the point of view of a natural person who is reasonably well-informed, observant and circumspect, taking into account the circumstances and the context of use. This obligation shall not apply to AI systems authorised by law to detect, prevent, investigate or prosecute criminal offences, subject to appropriate safeguards for the rights and freedoms of third parties, unless those systems are available for the public to report a criminal offence.  
2. Providers of AI systems, including general-purpose AI systems, generating synthetic audio, image, video or text content, shall ensure that the outputs of the AI system are marked in a machine-readable format and detectable as artificially generated or manipulated. Providers shall ensure their technical solutions are effective, interoperable, robust and reliable as far as this is technically feasible, taking into account the specificities and limitations of various types of content, the costs of implementation and the generally acknowledged state of the art, as may be reflected in relevant technical standards.  
3. Deployers of an emotion recognition system or a biometric categorisation system shall inform the natural persons exposed thereto of the operation of the system, and shall process the personal data in accordance with Regulations (EU) 2016/679 and (EU) 2018/1725 and Directive (EU) 2016/680, as applicable.  
4. Deployers of an AI system that generates or manipulates image, audio or video content constituting a deep fake, shall disclose that the content has been artificially generated or manipulated. This obligation shall not apply where the use is authorised by law to detect, prevent, investigate or prosecute criminal offence.  
Deployers of an AI system that generates or manipulates text which is published with the purpose of informing the public on matters of public interest shall disclose that the text has been artificially generated or manipulated.  
5. The information referred to in paragraphs 1 to 4 shall be provided to the natural persons concerned in a clear and distinguishable manner at the latest at the time of the first interaction or exposure.  
**Financial AI note (G3):** Customer-facing banking chatbots must disclose AI interaction at the start of the first interaction (§1). AI-generated content must be machine-readable marked (§2). Applies from August 2026 — aligns with Korean AI Basic Act Article 31 disclosure requirements for parallel compliance design.  
---
response:
### Answer to AI Governance Question on Whistleblowing Framework

**Regulatory Context**: Article 87 of the EU AI Act establishes protections for individuals reporting infringements of the AI Act, fostering a culture of accountability and transparency within organizations, including financial institutions.

#### Governance Axis: G4 (Compliance)

#### Impact on AI Governance Culture:
1. **Encouragement of Reporting**: The whistleblowing framework encourages employees and stakeholders to report potential violations without fear of retaliation. This can lead to a more proactive approach to compliance and risk management within the bank's AI governance culture.
  
2. **Enhanced Transparency**: By protecting whistleblowers, the bank can cultivate an environment where concerns regarding AI system compliance, safety, and ethical use are openly discussed, thereby enhancing overall transparency in AI operations.

3. **Accountability Mechanism**: The framework serves as an accountability mechanism, ensuring that all employees understand their role in maintaining compliance with the AI Act and that there are consequences for non-compliance.

#### Obligations Created:
1. **Protection of Whistleblowers**: The bank must implement policies that protect individuals who report infringements from retaliation, ensuring that they can report concerns safely and confidentially.

2. **Awareness and Training**: The bank is obligated to inform employees about the whistleblowing process and the protections available under Article 87. This may involve training sessions and clear communication of the procedures for reporting.

3. **Monitoring and Reporting Mechanisms**: The bank should establish and maintain effective internal reporting mechanisms that allow for the safe and anonymous reporting of AI-related compliance issues, as well as procedures for investigating these reports.

4. **Integration with Existing Compliance Frameworks**: The obligations under Article 87 should be integrated into the bank's existing compliance frameworks, ensuring that AI governance policies align with broader regulatory requirements and internal governance arrangements.

#### High-Risk Scenarios:
- **Failure to Protect Whistleblowers**: Not adequately protecting whistleblowers could lead to a culture of fear, discouraging reporting and increasing the risk of non-compliance with the AI Act.
- **Inadequate Reporting Mechanisms**: If the bank lacks effective reporting mechanisms, it may miss critical compliance issues, leading to potential regulatory penalties.

#### Recommendation for Human Oversight:
- **Oversight of Reporting Mechanisms**: Assign a dedicated compliance officer or team to oversee the whistleblowing process, ensuring that reports are handled appropriately and that there is a clear follow-up on any issues raised.

In summary, the whistleblowing framework under Article 87 of the EU AI Act significantly influences the bank's AI governance culture by promoting transparency, accountability, and compliance, while creating specific obligations that must be integrated into the bank's operational practices.

question: The European AI Board established under Articles 65-66 will issue opinions on EU AI Act interpretation. How should the AI Review Committee monitor and incorporate Board opinions into its governance framework?
ground_truth: Articles 65 and 66 establish the European AI Board and its functions: (1) Board composition (Art.65) — composed of one representative from each Member State's national competent authority; Commission representative; EU AI Office may participate; (2) Board functions (Art.66) — the Board: advises and assists the Commission in implementation; collects and shares expertise; contributes to uniform application across Member States; issues opinions and recommendations; (3) Relevance for financial AI — the Board will issue opinions on: interpretation of 'High-Risk AI' classification criteria (affecting Annex III §5 financial AI); implementation guidance for Art.9 risk management; Art.10 data governance standards; human oversight models for specific High-Risk AI categories; (4) Legal status of Board opinions — Board opinions and recommendations do not have binding legal force but carry strong interpretive authority; national competent authorities are likely to follow Board guidance in enforcement decisions; (5) AI Review Committee monitoring obligations: subscribe to AI Office and Board publications and consultations; designate a regulatory intelligence function monitoring Board activities; participate in public consultations on Board opinions related to financial AI; (6) Implementation of Board opinions: when the Board issues guidance on financial AI High-Risk classification or compliance standards, the committee should assess current practices against the opinion and implement changes within a reasonable compliance timeline; (7) Industry engagement — financial sector associations (EBF, Insurance Europe, EFAMA) participate in Board advisory processes; the bank should monitor and contribute to these association positions; (8) Connection to Art.96 Commission guidelines — the Board feeds into Commission implementation guidelines (Art.96) that provide additional compliance clarity.
response:
### Monitoring and Incorporating European AI Board Opinions into Governance Framework

**1. Governance Axis: G4 (Compliance)**  
The incorporation of opinions from the European AI Board is crucial for ensuring compliance with the EU AI Act.

**2. Establish a Monitoring Mechanism**  
- **Regular Review Schedule**: The AI Review Committee should establish a regular schedule (e.g., quarterly) to review opinions issued by the European AI Board under Articles 65-66. This ensures timely incorporation of new interpretations and guidance.
- **Designated Compliance Officer**: Appoint a compliance officer responsible for tracking and disseminating relevant opinions to the AI Review Committee and other stakeholders within the institution.

**3. Update Compliance Checklists**  
- **Mapping to Article 16 Obligations**: As the opinions may impact the obligations outlined in Article 16, the AI Review Committee should update its compliance checklist to reflect any changes or clarifications provided by the Board. This includes ensuring that all obligations (e.g., quality management systems, technical documentation) are aligned with the latest interpretations.

**4. Training and Awareness**  
- **Internal Training Programs**: Conduct training sessions for relevant staff on the implications of the Board's opinions, particularly those affecting high-risk AI systems as defined in Article 43 and Article 16. This will enhance understanding and compliance across the organization.

**5. Documentation and Reporting**  
- **Maintain Records**: Document all opinions received and the actions taken in response. This will serve as evidence of compliance efforts and facilitate audits by national competent authorities as required under Article 16(j).
- **Reporting to Senior Management**: Regularly report findings and updates from the European AI Board to senior management to ensure alignment with the institution's strategic objectives and compliance posture.

**6. Human Oversight in High-Risk Scenarios**  
- **Flag High-Risk Scenarios**: If any opinion suggests a significant change in compliance requirements or introduces new risks, the AI Review Committee should flag these scenarios for immediate review and consider implementing human oversight mechanisms to mitigate potential risks.

By following these structured steps, the AI Review Committee can effectively monitor and incorporate the European AI Board's opinions into its governance framework, ensuring ongoing compliance with the EU AI Act.

question: Annex III §5(a) classifies AI evaluating eligibility for essential public services as High-Risk. The bank administers government-backed mortgage guarantee programs using AI. Does this qualify?
ground_truth: Annex III §5(a) covers: 'AI systems intended to be used by public authorities or on behalf of public authorities to evaluate the eligibility of natural persons for essential public assistance benefits and services, including healthcare services, as well as to grant, reduce, revoke, or reclaim such benefits and services.' For bank administration of government-backed mortgage guarantee programs: (1) 'By or on behalf of public authorities' — this is the critical jurisdictional element; if the bank administers the guarantee program as an agent of a public authority (government), the AI may qualify under §5(a); if the bank independently uses AI for its own credit decisions on government-backed products, §5(a) may not apply (but §5(b) creditworthiness evaluation still applies); (2) 'Essential public assistance benefits and services' — government-backed mortgage guarantees are a form of public assistance enabling housing access; if the AI determines whether an applicant qualifies for the guarantee program (and thus for affordable mortgage access), this is evaluating eligibility for an essential public benefit; (3) Dual classification possible — a government-backed mortgage AI could potentially qualify under both §5(a) (if evaluating public guarantee eligibility as public authority agent) and §5(b) (creditworthiness evaluation); both classifications require the same Chapter III compliance; (4) Practical determination — the bank should review the contractual arrangements with the government authority: if the bank acts as government agent in administering the program, §5(a) applies; if the bank independently offers government-backed mortgages, §5(b) applies; (5) Same compliance obligations either way — whether §5(a) or §5(b) applies, the result is High-Risk AI with full Chapter III obligations; the classification question primarily affects which regulator has oversight.
legal_basis: EU AI Act Annex III §5(a); Article 6(2)
response:
### Answer

**Regulatory Context:**
According to Annex III §5(a), AI systems intended to be used by public authorities or on behalf of public authorities to evaluate the eligibility of natural persons for essential public assistance benefits and services are classified as High-Risk AI systems.

**Analysis:**
1. **Nature of the AI System**: The bank administers government-backed mortgage guarantee programs using AI. This involves evaluating the eligibility of individuals for a government-backed service (mortgage guarantees).
  
2. **Classification**: Since the AI system is used to evaluate eligibility for a public service (mortgage guarantees), it falls under the classification outlined in Annex III §5(a). Therefore, it qualifies as a High-Risk AI system.

**Governance Axis**: G4 (Compliance)

**Recommendations**:
- **Human Oversight**: Implement human oversight measures as per Article 14 to mitigate risks associated with the use of this High-Risk AI system. This includes ensuring that personnel can:
  - Understand the AI's capabilities and limitations (Article 14(4)(a)).
  - Resist automation bias (Article 14(4)(b)).
  - Correctly interpret the AI's output (Article 14(4)(c)).
  - Override AI recommendations (Article 14(4)(d)).
  - Stop the system if necessary (Article 14(4)(e)).
  
- **Compliance Checklist**: The AI Review Committee should develop a compliance checklist aligned with Article 16 obligations for High-Risk AI systems, ensuring all necessary measures are in place before deployment.

**High-Risk Scenario**: The use of AI in evaluating eligibility for government-backed mortgage guarantees presents a high-risk scenario due to its potential impact on individuals' access to essential services. Therefore, robust human oversight is essential.

question: Article 72(4) requires deployers to report relevant data to providers to support post-market monitoring. What data must the bank (as deployer) provide to its credit scoring AI vendor, and how should this data-sharing relationship be structured?
ground_truth: Article 72(4) states: 'Where deployers have concluded a service level agreement or another contractual arrangement with providers of High-Risk AI systems, the relevant data required for the post-market monitoring under paragraph 3 shall be provided by deployers to the provider.' For the bank as deployer sharing monitoring data with the vendor-provider: (1) Data that must be shared — data relevant to the post-market monitoring plan (Art.72(3)) which the provider uses to assess ongoing AI performance; for credit scoring AI, this includes: model input data characteristics (distribution of applicant profiles over time); model output distributions (score distributions over time); credit decision outcomes over time (performance of AI-approved vs AI-rejected applicants, where available); customer complaint and appeal data related to AI decisions; human override rates and patterns; any serious incidents related to the AI; (2) Privacy and data protection — the data-sharing arrangement must comply with GDPR; personal data shared with the vendor requires: legal basis (typically contract performance or legitimate interests); data processing agreement with the vendor; appropriate security measures; data minimization (aggregate statistics where possible rather than individual applicant data); (3) Contractual structure — the SLA or vendor contract should include: specific data points to be reported; reporting frequency (monthly aggregate statistics; immediate notification of serious incidents); data format and transmission security specifications; vendor's obligations to maintain data confidentiality; (4) Aggregate vs. individual data — prefer aggregate statistical data over individual applicant data to minimize privacy risk while satisfying monitoring needs; (5) AI Review Committee obligation — include Art.72(4) data-sharing requirements in vendor contract template; verify data-sharing arrangements are GDPR-compliant before execution.
response:
### Answer to AI Governance Question

**Governance Axis:** G4 (Compliance)

**Data to be Provided by the Bank (Deployer) to the Credit Scoring AI Vendor (Provider):**

1. **Performance Monitoring Data:**
   - The bank must provide data related to the operational performance of the credit scoring AI system, including any deviations from expected outcomes as per the instructions for use (Article 26(5)).
   - This includes metrics on accuracy, robustness, and any incidents that may indicate risks to health, safety, or fundamental rights (Article 13(3)(b)(ii) and Article 26(5)).

2. **Incident Reports:**
   - Any serious incidents or risks identified during the operation of the AI system must be reported to the provider without undue delay (Article 26(5)).
   - This includes information on any identified risks that may affect the AI system's compliance with its intended purpose.

3. **Input Data Relevance:**
   - The bank should share insights on the relevance and representativeness of the input data used in the AI system, especially if the bank exercises control over this data (Article 26(4)).

4. **User Feedback:**
   - Feedback from users (e.g., loan officers) regarding the AI system's outputs and usability should be communicated to the provider to facilitate improvements and adjustments (Article 26(5)).

**Structure of the Data-Sharing Relationship:**

1. **Formal Agreement:**
   - Establish a formal data-sharing agreement that outlines the types of data to be shared, the frequency of reporting, and the responsibilities of both parties in maintaining data confidentiality and security.

2. **Regular Reporting Schedule:**
   - Implement a regular reporting schedule (e.g., monthly or quarterly) to ensure timely sharing of performance metrics and incident reports.

3. **Data Security Measures:**
   - Ensure that both parties adhere to data protection regulations, including GDPR compliance, when sharing personal data or sensitive information (Article 26(9)).

4. **Human Oversight:**
   - Assign competent personnel to oversee the data-sharing process, ensuring that the information shared is accurate and relevant (Article 26(2)).

5. **Feedback Mechanism:**
   - Create a feedback mechanism for the bank's users to report their experiences and insights regarding the AI system, which can be relayed to the provider for continuous improvement.

By adhering to these guidelines, the bank can ensure compliance with Article 72(4) and maintain a productive relationship with the credit scoring AI vendor.

question: The bank's marketing AI creates comprehensive 'financial wellness scores' combining credit data, social media activity, purchase patterns, and behavioral data to predict customer product needs. The AI Review Committee must assess whether this approaches the Article 5(1)(c) social scoring prohibition.
ground_truth: Article 5(1)(c) prohibits: 'AI systems evaluating or classifying natural persons or groups of persons over a period of time based on their social behaviour or known or predicted personal or professional characteristics, with the social score leading to either or both of the following: (i) detrimental or unfavourable treatment of certain natural persons or groups of persons in social contexts that are unrelated to the contexts in which the data was originally generated or collected; (ii) detrimental or unfavourable treatment of certain natural persons or groups of persons that is unjustified or disproportionate to their social behaviour or its gravity.' For the comprehensive financial wellness score: (1) 'Social behaviour' component — incorporating social media activity, purchase patterns, and behavioral data goes beyond financial creditworthiness data; these elements reflect 'social behaviour' and 'personal characteristics'; this is a significant red flag for social scoring proximity; (2) 'Over a period of time' — longitudinal scoring based on behavioral patterns over time is a characteristic of social scoring systems; (3) 'Detrimental treatment in unrelated contexts' — if the financial wellness score influences credit decisions based on social media behavior or lifestyle choices unrelated to actual credit risk, this creates treatment in financial contexts based on data generated in social contexts; this satisfies Art.5(1)(c)(i); (4) 'Unjustified or disproportionate' — using social media data to determine financial product offers without demonstrated actuarial connection to financial risk may be disproportionate; (5) Private sector question — Art.5(1)(c) explicitly states this prohibition applies to AI systems operated by public authorities; a private bank's comprehensive scoring system may not fall within the prohibition's literal scope (targeting public authority social scoring); however, the financial regulator and AI supervisory authority may view comprehensive private sector behavioral scoring as approaching the prohibited practice; (6) Strong caution — the AI Review Committee should require rigorous legal analysis before approving any AI system combining social behavioral data with financial decision-making.
context_used:
### Article 6 — Classification Rules for High-Risk AI Systems  
**Chapter III, Section 1: Classification of AI Systems as High-Risk**
**Date of application:** 2 August 2026 (Article 113)  
1. Irrespective of whether an AI system is placed on the market or put into service independently from the products referred to in points (a) and (b), that AI system shall be considered to be high-risk where both of the following conditions are fulfilled:
(a) the AI system is intended to be used as a safety component of a product, or the AI system is itself a product, covered by the Union harmonisation legislation listed in Annex I;
(b) the product whose safety component pursuant to point (a) is the AI system, or the AI system itself as a product, is required to undergo a third-party conformity assessment with a view to the placing on the market or putting into service of that product pursuant to the Union harmonisation legislation listed in Annex I.  
2. In addition to the high-risk AI systems referred to in paragraph 1, AI systems referred to in Annex III shall also be considered to be high-risk.  
3. By derogation from paragraph 2, an AI system referred to in Annex III shall not be considered to be high-risk where it does not pose a significant risk of harm to the health, safety or fundamental rights of natural persons, including by not materially influencing the outcome of decision making.  
An AI system referred to in Annex III shall not be considered to be high-risk if it fulfils any of the following conditions:
(a) the AI system is intended to perform a narrow procedural task;
(b) the AI system is intended to improve the result of a previously completed human activity;
(c) the AI system is intended to detect decision-making patterns or deviations from prior decision-making patterns and is not meant to replace or influence the previously completed human assessment, without proper human review;
(d) the AI system is intended to perform a preparatory task to an assessment relevant for the purposes of the use cases listed in Annex III.  
**Financial AI note (G4):** Credit scoring AI (Annex III §5(b)) and insurance pricing AI (Annex III §5(c)) are definitively High-Risk. The Article 6(3) narrow exception does NOT apply to credit scoring AI that directly influences credit decisions.  
---  
### Annex III — High-Risk AI Systems (Article 6(2))  
**Financial sector relevant classifications:**  
**Point 4 — Employment, workers management and access to self-employment:**
(a) AI systems intended to be used for the recruitment or selection of natural persons, notably for placing targeted job advertisements, for analysing and filtering job applications, and for evaluating candidates;
(b) AI systems intended to be used for making decisions on promotion and termination of work-related contractual relationships, for allocating tasks based on individual behaviour or personal traits or characteristics or for monitoring and evaluating performance and behaviour of persons in such relationships.  
**Point 5 — Access to and enjoyment of essential private services and essential public services and benefits:**
(a) AI systems intended to be used by public authorities or on behalf of public authorities to evaluate the eligibility of natural persons for essential public assistance benefits and services, including healthcare services, adequate housing and electricity, and to grant, reduce, revoke, or reclaim such benefits and services;
(b) AI systems intended to be used to evaluate the creditworthiness of natural persons or establish their credit score, **with the exception of AI systems used for the purpose of detecting financial fraud**;
(c) AI systems intended to be used for risk assessment and pricing in relation to natural persons in the case of life and health insurance;
(d) AI systems intended to be used for dispatching, or establishing priority in the dispatching of emergency first response services, including by police, firefighters and medical aid, as well as of emergency healthcare patient triage systems.  
**Financial AI classification summary:**
- Credit scoring AI → **High-Risk** (§5(b)) — lending domain
- Life/health insurance pricing AI → **High-Risk** (§5(c)) — insurance domain
- Financial fraud detection AI → **NOT High-Risk** (§5(b) explicit exception)
- Robo-advisory AI → NOT currently High-Risk (no explicit Annex III category)
- AML/KYC AI (private bank) → NOT High-Risk (§6 limited to law enforcement)
- Employment/recruitment AI → **High-Risk** (§4(a))  
---

---

### Article 5 — Prohibited AI Practices  
**Chapter II: Prohibited AI Practices**
**Date of application:** 2 February 2025 (Article 113(a))  
1. The following AI practices shall be prohibited:  
(a) the placing on the market, the putting into service or the use of an AI system that deploys subliminal techniques beyond a person's consciousness or purposefully manipulative or deceptive techniques, with the objective of, or with the effect of materially distorting the behaviour of a person or a group of persons by appreciably impairing their ability to make an informed decision, thereby causing them to take a decision that they would not have otherwise taken in a manner that causes or is reasonably likely to cause that person, another person or a group of persons significant harm;  
(b) the placing on the market, the putting into service or the use of an AI system that exploits any of the vulnerabilities of a natural person or a specific group of persons due to their age, disability or a specific social or economic situation, with the objective of, or the effect of, materially distorting the behaviour of that person or a group of persons in a manner that causes or is reasonably likely to cause that person, another person or a group of persons significant harm;  
(c) the placing on the market, the putting into service or the use of AI systems for the evaluation or classification of natural persons or groups of persons over a certain period of time based on their social behaviour or known, inferred or predicted personal or personality characteristics, with the social score leading to either or both of the following: (i) detrimental or unfavourable treatment of certain natural persons or groups of persons in social contexts that are unrelated to the contexts in which the data was originally generated or collected; (ii) detrimental or unfavourable treatment of certain natural persons or groups of persons that is unjustified or disproportionate to their social behaviour or its gravity;  
(f) the placing on the market, the putting into service or the use of AI systems used for making inferences about natural persons on the basis of their biometric data in order to categorise them according to race, political opinions, trade union membership, religious or philosophical beliefs, sex life or sexual orientation; this prohibition shall not apply to labelling or filtering of lawfully acquired biometric datasets or in law enforcement by competent authorities for the categorisation of biometric data;  
(g) the placing on the market, the putting into service or the use of facial recognition systems for law enforcement purposes, unless strictly necessary for a targeted search of a specific victim or to prevent a specific, substantial and imminent threat;  
(h) the placing on the market, the putting into service, or the use, for law enforcement purposes, of AI systems for making risk assessments of natural persons in order to assess the risk of a natural person for offending or reoffending, or for predicting the occurrence or recurrence of an actual or potential criminal offence.  
**Financial AI note (G2):** Article 5 prohibitions apply as of February 2025. Financial AI must not use subliminal manipulation (5(a)), exploit vulnerable customers (5(b)), or implement social scoring systems (5(c)). The AI Review Committee must screen all AI systems for prohibited practices before approval.  
---

---

### Article 14 — Human Oversight  
**Chapter III, Section 2: Requirements for High-Risk AI Systems**
**Date of application:** 2 August 2026 (Article 113)  
1. High-risk AI systems shall be designed and developed in such a way, including with appropriate human-machine interface tools, that they can be effectively overseen by natural persons during the period in which they are in use.  
2. Human oversight shall aim to prevent or minimise the risks to health, safety or fundamental rights that may emerge when a high-risk AI system is used in accordance with its intended purpose or under conditions of reasonably foreseeable misuse, in particular where such risks persist despite the application of other requirements set out in this Section.  
3. The oversight measures shall be commensurate with the risks, level of autonomy and context of use of the high-risk AI system, and shall be ensured through either one or both of the following types of measures:
(a) measures identified and built, when technically feasible, into the high-risk AI system by the provider before it is placed on the market or put into service;
(b) measures identified by the provider before placing the high-risk AI system on the market or putting it into service and that are appropriate to be implemented by the deployer.  
4. For the purpose of implementing paragraphs 1, 2 and 3, the high-risk AI system shall be provided to the deployer in such a way that natural persons to whom human oversight is assigned are enabled, as appropriate and proportionate:
(a) to properly understand the relevant capacities and limitations of the high-risk AI system and be able to duly monitor its operation, including in view of detecting and addressing anomalies, dysfunctions and unexpected performance;
(b) to remain aware of the possible tendency of automatically relying or over-relying on the output produced by a high-risk AI system (automation bias), in particular for high-risk AI systems used to provide information or recommendations for decisions to be taken by natural persons;
(c) to correctly interpret the high-risk AI system's output, taking into account, for example, the interpretation tools and methods available;
(d) to decide, in any particular situation, not to use the high-risk AI system or to otherwise disregard, override or reverse the output of the high-risk AI system;
(e) to intervene in the operation of the high-risk AI system or interrupt the system through a 'stop' button or a similar procedure that allows the system to come to a halt in a safe state.  
5. For high-risk AI systems referred to in point 1(a) of Annex III, the measures referred to in paragraph 3 of this Article shall be such as to ensure that, in addition, no action or decision is taken by the deployer on the basis of the identification resulting from the system unless that identification has been separately verified and confirmed by at least two natural persons with the necessary competence, training and authority.  
**Financial AI note (G2):** Loan officers using credit scoring AI must be enabled to: understand AI capabilities/limitations (§4(a)); resist automation bias (§4(b)); correctly interpret AI output (§4(c)); override AI recommendations (§4(d)); and stop the system if necessary (§4(e)). All five capabilities must be documented and trained.  
---
response:
### Assessment of Marketing AI and Article 5(1)(c) Compliance

**1. Regulatory Context:**
   - Article 5(1)(c) prohibits the use of AI systems for the evaluation or classification of natural persons based on their social behaviour or known, inferred, or predicted personal characteristics, leading to detrimental treatment.

**2. Analysis of the Marketing AI:**
   - The bank's marketing AI creates 'financial wellness scores' by combining various data sources, including credit data, social media activity, purchase patterns, and behavioral data.
   - This scoring system aims to predict customer product needs, which may involve evaluating customers based on their financial behavior and personal characteristics.

**3. Potential Compliance Issues:**
   - The use of social media activity and behavioral data could be interpreted as evaluating or classifying individuals based on their social behavior and personal characteristics.
   - If the financial wellness scores lead to detrimental treatment of customers (e.g., denying access to products or services based on their score), it could fall under the prohibition outlined in Article 5(1)(c).

**4. Governance Axis:**
   - This scenario primarily relates to **G4 (Compliance)**, as it involves adherence to regulatory prohibitions.

**5. High-Risk Scenario Flag:**
   - The potential for the marketing AI to classify individuals in a manner that could lead to significant harm or unfair treatment raises a high-risk scenario.
   - The AI Review Committee should be particularly cautious about how these scores are used in decision-making processes.

**6. Recommendation for Human Oversight:**
   - Implement robust human oversight measures to ensure that the outputs of the marketing AI are reviewed by qualified personnel before any decisions are made based on the financial wellness scores.
   - Ensure that the oversight includes:
     - Understanding the AI's capabilities and limitations (Article 14(4)(a)).
     - Monitoring for automation bias (Article 14(4)(b)).
     - Correct interpretation of AI outputs (Article 14(4)(c)).
     - The ability to override AI recommendations (Article 14(4)(d)).
     - A mechanism to halt the AI system if necessary (Article 14(4)(e)).

**7. Conclusion:**
   - The AI Review Committee must assess the specific implementation and use of the financial wellness scores to determine compliance with Article 5(1)(c). If the AI system is found to classify individuals in a manner that could lead to unfair treatment, it may need to be re-evaluated or modified to ensure compliance with the prohibition against social scoring.

question: The Korean bank's AI team in Seoul develops a credit scoring AI deployed for its EU customers. Article 22 requires providers established outside the EU to designate an authorised representative. What must this representative do?
ground_truth: Article 22 requires that providers of High-Risk AI systems established in third countries (including Korea) must, prior to making their High-Risk AI systems available on the EU market, designate in writing an authorised representative established in the Union. Obligations and structure: (1) Designation requirement — the authorised representative must be established in the Union (EU Member State); a subsidiary, European legal entity, or contracted EU-based firm can serve; (2) Authorised representative mandate (Art.22(2)) — the representative must be authorised to perform on behalf of the provider: compliance with the obligations of the provider under the EU AI Act; cooperation with national competent authorities; registration in the EU database on behalf of the provider; actions related to market surveillance; (3) Mandate scope (Art.22(3)) — the written mandate must specify: the AI systems covered; the representative's authority to act on behalf of the provider; contact information for competent authority interactions; (4) Compliance responsibilities — the authorised representative can be held jointly liable with the provider for non-compliance; this creates a direct EU regulatory interface; (5) Practical options for the Korean bank: EU banking subsidiary — the bank's existing EU banking subsidiary can be designated as authorised representative; EU law firm — law firms with EU AI Act expertise can serve as authorised representatives; EU AI compliance specialist — dedicated third-party authorised representative services are emerging; (6) The authorised representative must have: access to the AI system's technical documentation; ability to respond promptly to competent authority requests; authority to take corrective actions on behalf of the Korean provider; (7) Registration — the authorised representative's details must be included in the EU database registration (Art.49) and in the EU Declaration of Conformity (Art.47).
response:
### Answer to AI Governance Question

**Governance Axis:** G4 (Compliance)

**Context:** The Korean bank's AI team in Seoul is developing a credit scoring AI for EU customers, which falls under the scope of the regulation due to its extra-territorial application (Article 2(1)(c)).

**Obligations of the Authorised Representative (Article 22):**
1. **Act on Behalf of the Provider:** The authorised representative must act on behalf of the provider (the Korean bank) in the EU. This includes handling communications with national competent authorities and ensuring compliance with the regulation.
   
2. **Ensure Compliance:** The representative is responsible for ensuring that the high-risk AI system complies with the requirements set out in the regulation, particularly those outlined in Article 16 regarding obligations of providers of high-risk AI systems.

3. **Registration Assistance:** The authorised representative must assist the provider in fulfilling the registration obligations as specified in Article 49, ensuring that the AI system is registered in the EU database before it is placed on the market or put into service.

4. **Documentation and Information:** The representative should maintain access to the technical documentation and any other relevant information required to demonstrate compliance with the regulation, as stipulated in Article 16.

5. **Point of Contact:** They serve as the point of contact for any inquiries or requests from EU authorities regarding the AI system.

**Recommendation for Human Oversight:**
- Given the high-risk nature of credit scoring AI, it is advisable to have human oversight in the compliance processes to ensure that all obligations are met and to mitigate risks associated with regulatory enforcement.

**Actionable Steps:**
- The Korean bank should designate an authorised representative in the EU as per Article 22 and ensure that this representative is well-informed about the compliance requirements and obligations under the regulation.

question: The bank discovers that sophisticated applicants are gaming the credit scoring AI by strategically arranging their financial data to achieve artificially high scores. How does Article 15 of the EU AI Act address adversarial robustness, and what must the committee do?
ground_truth: Article 15(1) requires High-Risk AI systems to achieve, in light of their intended purpose, an appropriate level of accuracy, robustness and cybersecurity, and to be resilient with regard to errors, faults or inconsistencies that may occur within the system or the environment in which the system operates, in particular due to interactions with natural persons or other systems. For the credit scoring AI gaming scenario: (1) Adversarial robustness requirement — Art.15(1) references resilience against 'attempts by third parties to alter the use, outputs or performance of the High-Risk AI system'; deliberately gaming the AI by manipulating financial data inputs is precisely such an attempt; (2) Required response — the provider/deployer must: assess the vulnerability (how many applicants are gaming and by how much?); implement technical countermeasures: adversarial training — incorporate adversarial examples into training to improve model robustness; anomaly detection — flag unusual input patterns that may indicate gaming; velocity monitoring — detect rapid changes in financial behavior preceding loan applications; consistency checks — validate financial data across multiple sources; (3) Balance with legitimate behavior — the committee must distinguish between: fraudulent gaming — fabricating financial data (criminal); legitimate optimization — improving genuine financial behavior before applying (acceptable); borderline cases — arranging finances optimally within legitimate bounds; (4) Art.9 risk management update — add 'adversarial input gaming' as an identified risk to the Art.9 risk management system; document mitigation measures; (5) Art.10 data governance — assess whether adversarial examples should be incorporated into training data to improve robustness (Art.10 data governance applies to any training data changes); (6) Technical documentation update — document the vulnerability and mitigation approach in the Annex IV technical documentation.
